questions.question: What is the function of an acoustic model in automatic speech recognition, and how do deep neural networks improve its performance?
keyPoints: Acoustic model maps acoustic features (e.g., filterbank, MFCC) to phonetic states or context-dependent phones.
Deep neural networks replace traditional GMMs, providing better modeling of complex acoustic distributions.
DNNs can exploit context-dependent phone representations (triphones) and learn hierarchical features.
Training uses cross-entropy or CTC loss; DNNs improve accuracy and robustness to noise.

questions.question: Explain the role of a language model in speech recognition and describe a neural network-based language model architecture.
keyPoints: Language model estimates the probability of a word sequence, guiding the decoder to produce fluent text.
Neural LMs (e.g., RNN, LSTM, Transformer) learn context representations and outperform n-gram LMs.
They are integrated with acoustic scores during beam‑search decoding using weighted sum (acoustic + LM).
Training minimizes perplexity; LM scoring reduces word error rate by penalising unlikely sentences.

questions.question: Describe the architecture of an end‑to‑end automatic speech recognition (ASR) system. Compare CTC‑based and attention‑based encoder‑decoder models, discussing how they handle alignment and their respective advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: End‑to‑end ASR directly maps input audio spectrograms to output text, removing explicit phonetic lexicons.
CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification) uses a frame‑wise encoder with a blank symbol to handle variable‑length alignments, assuming conditional independence.
Attention‑based encoder‑decoder learns soft alignment between input frames and output tokens via attention, allowing longer‑range dependencies.

CTC is simpler and works well for streaming, but struggles with long sequences; attention yields higher accuracy on varied data but is computationally heavier and less suitable for real‑time inference.

questions.question: Discuss the decoding algorithms used in ASR, covering greedy decoding versus beam search and the integration of language models. Additionally, evaluate how transformer‑based language models improve ASR performance.
keyPoints: Decoding searches for the most probable word sequence given acoustic and language model scores.
Greedy decoding selects the highest‑probability token at each step, fast but suboptimal; beam search keeps the top‑k hypotheses, exploring many paths.
LM integration is performed by adding log‑probability of the LM term (weighted by λ) to the acoustic score, often with insertion penalties.

Transformer LMs capture long‑range context via self‑attention, yielding lower perplexity; shallow‑fusion or deep‑fusion with ASR improves word error rate, especially in noisy or low‑resource conditions.
